title: Cost and profit negotiations - Analyze and negotiate costs | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Getting started with cost and profit negotiations

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Cost and profit negotiations - Analyze and negotiate costs
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Cost and profit negotiations - Analyze and negotiate costs
structure_blocks.text: Analyze and negotiate costs There are two steps in building a negotiated contract price. The first one is to establish a cost base, and the second one is to determine the profit. Establishing a cost base involves determining whether estimated or actual costs are fair and reasonable in accordance with: Contract Cost Principles 1031-2, section 5.1.0 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Costing Standard criteria of attributable, appropriate and reasonable Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission, and Costing discussion papers (see Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 4.3) Commercial pricing Cost-based Pricing Principles are not required for commercial goods and services, since these are used regularly for non-government purposes, and are sold by the offeror in the course of carrying out its normal business operations; and there is a sufficient number of buyers, other than the government, to establish a relevant market price for the good or service. Sufficient price support for a commercial price must be available to support a commercial price or Cost-based Pricing Principles will apply. (Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.0.2, Commercial Pricing). Contract cost principles Whenever a contract price is negotiated based on costs, the costs will be determined using Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 . For non-competitive contracts valued at $50,000 and over, with a fixed price or fixed time rate basis of payment, except in cases for the acquisition of commercial goods and services, the price or rate will be negotiated based on the estimated costs computed in accordance with the Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 . For non-competitive contracts valued at $50,000 or over, with a cost reimbursable basis of payment, except in cases for the acquisition of commercial goods and services, the price will be determined based on actual ¬costs incurred computed in accordance with the Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 . In both of the above cases, the Contract Cost Principles will be included as a condition of the contract. Costing Standard The Costing Standard is located in Section 5.1.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . It supplements Contract Cost Principles by applying principles-based and risk-guided approaches. This facilitates consistency, improves understanding and supports buyers’ professional judgement in determining fair, reasonable, and effective pricing. Criteria for cost acceptability For the cost to be acceptable, the contract cost must be attributable, appropriate and reasonable. A cost is… If it is... attributable incurred directly or indirectly for the fulfillment of the contract, and necessary to fulfil the contract requirements appropriate by its character and nature, representing a cost that is expected to be incurred in the conduct of delivering the contract reasonable by its nature, not exceeding what might be expected to be incurred in the normal delivery of the contract, whether under a competitive or non-competitive procurement Cost categories and items The following tables detail specific cost items categorized by cost group, along with their associated subsections within Section 5.1.1, Costing Standard of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . Within the guide subsections, you will find descriptions of each cost item and information on how to assess its acceptability. Table 1: Asset-Based Costs Cost Category Cost Items Asset-Based Costs 1.1 Amortization of Unrealized Appreciation of Assets 1.2 Depreciation 1.3 Excess Production Capacity 1.4 Excess Facilities 1.5 Impairment of Assets 1.6 Leases 1.7 Special Production Tooling and Special Test Equipment 1.8 Government Assistance Related to Fixed Assets, Research and Product Development Table 2: Employed-Based Compensation Costs Cost Category Cost Items Employed-Based Compensation Costs 2.1 Dues and Membership 2.2 Downtime 2.3 Employee Benefits 2.4 Executive Benefits and Employee Compensation 2.5 Overtime Costs 2.6 Pension Costs 2.7 Pension Plan Refunds 2.8 Severance Payments 2.9 Training and Staff Development 2.10 Travel Costs and Living Expenses 2.11 Department of National Defence (DND) Facility Costs 2.12 Displacement/Dislocation Pay Allowance Table 3: Goods/Services-Based Costs Cost Category Cost Items Goods/Services-Based Costs 3.1 Bad Debts and Collection Charges 3.2 Inventory Losses and Obsolescence 3.3 Rework and Faulty Workmanship 3.4 Surplus Materials Expenses Table 4: Corporate/Various-Based Costs Cost Category Cost Items Corporate/Various-Based Costs 4.1 Allowance for Interest on Debt and Capital 4.2 Extraordinary or Unusual Matters or Events 4.3 Financial Related Expenses 4.4 Fines and Penalties 4.5 Fees for Professional Advice 4.6 Goodwill 4.7 Insurance 4.8 Legal, Accounting and Consulting Fees in Connection with Financial Reorganization, Security Issues, Capital Stock Issues, Obtaining of Patents and Licenses and Prosecution against the Crown 4.9 Losses on Investment 4.10 Losses on Other Contract 4.11 Patents and License 4.12 Prosecution of Claims against the Crown 4.13 Provisions for Contingencies and Warranty Costs 4.14 Refunds 4.15 Research and Development 4.16 Sales and Marketing Costs 4.17 Taxes 4.18 Joint Venture 4.19 Transfer Pricing 4.20 Head Office Expense 4.21 Environment Costs 4.22 Government Supplied Materials 4.23 Donations 4.24 Strategic Innovation Fund (SIF) Repayment Cost accounting practices submission Where a contract price will be based on estimated or actual costs, the buyer, in consultation with the Procurement Support Services Sector, may require a Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission evidenced by a CAP Submission Acceptance Letter to clarify the cost accounting practices to be used for contract pricing. The CAP Submission is a formal disclosure of a contractor’s cost accounting practices including the identification of direct and indirect costs and disclosure of methodologies used to allocate indirect costs. The CAP Submission and the CAP Submission Acceptance Letter (see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.1.2) ensure that the cost accounting practices to be used for contract pricing are clear and understood by all parties by fully disclosing the acceptance of the practices during the pre-contract award period. For additional information, see the following sections of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing : Section 5.1.2, Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission, and Annex 3, Process Steps for Costing Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission The CAP Submission serves to reinforce the contractor’s obligation to comply with Contract Cost Principles 1031-2. The CAP Submission does not address the reasonableness of the amount of costs but rather the nature of the cost or costing practices. Costing discussion papers The following discussion papers help explain costs and guide decisions about which costs are acceptable. They support you in understanding complex issues during contract negotiations and throughout the contract’s lifecycle. They can be found in Annex 4.3 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing : Annex 4.3.1, Sales and Marketing Annex 4.3.2, Asset Valuation Annex 4.3.3, Production Capacity and Indirect Costs Allocation Costing rates For methods of allocating the contractor’s fair and reasonable costs to a contract, refer to section 5.1.1.3, Contract Costing Rates of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , which covers: take-out rates out-of-plant service rates mobile repair party rates material handling rates purchased labour rates

raw_text:
Analyze and negotiate costs There are two steps in building a negotiated contract price. The first one is to establish a cost base, and the second one is to determine the profit. Establishing a cost base involves determining whether estimated or actual costs are fair and reasonable in accordance with: Contract Cost Principles 1031-2, section 5.1.0 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Costing Standard criteria of attributable, appropriate and reasonable Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission, and Costing discussion papers (see Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 4.3) Commercial pricing Cost-based Pricing Principles are not required for commercial goods and services, since these are used regularly for non-government purposes, and are sold by the offeror in the course of carrying out its normal business operations; and there is a sufficient number of buyers, other than the government, to establish a relevant market price for the good or service. Sufficient price support for a commercial price must be available to support a commercial price or Cost-based Pricing Principles will apply. (Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.0.2, Commercial Pricing). Contract cost principles Whenever a contract price is negotiated based on costs, the costs will be determined using Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 . For non-competitive contracts valued at $50,000 and over, with a fixed price or fixed time rate basis of payment, except in cases for the acquisition of commercial goods and services, the price or rate will be negotiated based on the estimated costs computed in accordance with the Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 . For non-competitive contracts valued at $50,000 or over, with a cost reimbursable basis of payment, except in cases for the acquisition of commercial goods and services, the price will be determined based on actual ¬costs incurred computed in accordance with the Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 . In both of the above cases, the Contract Cost Principles will be included as a condition of the contract. Costing Standard The Costing Standard is located in Section 5.1.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . It supplements Contract Cost Principles by applying principles-based and risk-guided approaches. This facilitates consistency, improves understanding and supports buyers’ professional judgement in determining fair, reasonable, and effective pricing. Criteria for cost acceptability For the cost to be acceptable, the contract cost must be attributable, appropriate and reasonable. A cost is… If it is... attributable incurred directly or indirectly for the fulfillment of the contract, and necessary to fulfil the contract requirements appropriate by its character and nature, representing a cost that is expected to be incurred in the conduct of delivering the contract reasonable by its nature, not exceeding what might be expected to be incurred in the normal delivery of the contract, whether under a competitive or non-competitive procurement Cost categories and items The following tables detail specific cost items categorized by cost group, along with their associated subsections within Section 5.1.1, Costing Standard of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . Within the guide subsections, you will find descriptions of each cost item and information on how to assess its acceptability. Table 1: Asset-Based Costs Cost Category Cost Items Asset-Based Costs 1.1 Amortization of Unrealized Appreciation of Assets 1.2 Depreciation 1.3 Excess Production Capacity 1.4 Excess Facilities 1.5 Impairment of Assets 1.6 Leases 1.7 Special Production Tooling and Special Test Equipment 1.8 Government Assistance Related to Fixed Assets, Research and Product Development Table 2: Employed-Based Compensation Costs Cost Category Cost Items Employed-Based Compensation Costs 2.1 Dues and Membership 2.2 Downtime 2.3 Employee Benefits 2.4 Executive Benefits and Employee Compensation 2.5 Overtime Costs 2.6 Pension Costs 2.7 Pension Plan Refunds 2.8 Severance Payments 2.9 Training and Staff Development 2.10 Travel Costs and Living Expenses 2.11 Department of National Defence (DND) Facility Costs 2.12 Displacement/Dislocation Pay Allowance Table 3: Goods/Services-Based Costs Cost Category Cost Items Goods/Services-Based Costs 3.1 Bad Debts and Collection Charges 3.2 Inventory Losses and Obsolescence 3.3 Rework and Faulty Workmanship 3.4 Surplus Materials Expenses Table 4: Corporate/Various-Based Costs Cost Category Cost Items Corporate/Various-Based Costs 4.1 Allowance for Interest on Debt and Capital 4.2 Extraordinary or Unusual Matters or Events 4.3 Financial Related Expenses 4.4 Fines and Penalties 4.5 Fees for Professional Advice 4.6 Goodwill 4.7 Insurance 4.8 Legal, Accounting and Consulting Fees in Connection with Financial Reorganization, Security Issues, Capital Stock Issues, Obtaining of Patents and Licenses and Prosecution against the Crown 4.9 Losses on Investment 4.10 Losses on Other Contract 4.11 Patents and License 4.12 Prosecution of Claims against the Crown 4.13 Provisions for Contingencies and Warranty Costs 4.14 Refunds 4.15 Research and Development 4.16 Sales and Marketing Costs 4.17 Taxes 4.18 Joint Venture 4.19 Transfer Pricing 4.20 Head Office Expense 4.21 Environment Costs 4.22 Government Supplied Materials 4.23 Donations 4.24 Strategic Innovation Fund (SIF) Repayment Cost accounting practices submission Where a contract price will be based on estimated or actual costs, the buyer, in consultation with the Procurement Support Services Sector, may require a Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission evidenced by a CAP Submission Acceptance Letter to clarify the cost accounting practices to be used for contract pricing. The CAP Submission is a formal disclosure of a contractor’s cost accounting practices including the identification of direct and indirect costs and disclosure of methodologies used to allocate indirect costs. The CAP Submission and the CAP Submission Acceptance Letter (see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.1.2) ensure that the cost accounting practices to be used for contract pricing are clear and understood by all parties by fully disclosing the acceptance of the practices during the pre-contract award period. For additional information, see the following sections of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing : Section 5.1.2, Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission, and Annex 3, Process Steps for Costing Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission The CAP Submission serves to reinforce the contractor’s obligation to comply with Contract Cost Principles 1031-2. The CAP Submission does not address the reasonableness of the amount of costs but rather the nature of the cost or costing practices. Costing discussion papers The following discussion papers help explain costs and guide decisions about which costs are acceptable. They support you in understanding complex issues during contract negotiations and throughout the contract’s lifecycle. They can be found in Annex 4.3 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing : Annex 4.3.1, Sales and Marketing Annex 4.3.2, Asset Valuation Annex 4.3.3, Production Capacity and Indirect Costs Allocation Costing rates For methods of allocating the contractor’s fair and reasonable costs to a contract, refer to section 5.1.1.3, Contract Costing Rates of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , which covers: take-out rates out-of-plant service rates mobile repair party rates material handling rates purchased labour rates

Analyze and negotiate costs

There are two steps in building a negotiated contract price. The first one is to establish a cost base, and the second one is to determine the profit. Establishing a cost base involves determining whether estimated or actual costs are fair and reasonable in accordance with:

Contract Cost Principles 1031-2, section 5.1.0 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

Costing Standard criteria of attributable, appropriate and reasonable

Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission, and

Costing discussion papers (see Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 4.3)

Commercial pricing

Cost-based Pricing Principles are not required for commercial goods and services, since these are used regularly for non-government purposes, and are sold by the offeror in the course of carrying out its normal business operations; and there is a sufficient number of buyers, other than the government, to establish a relevant market price for the good or service.

Sufficient price support for a commercial price must be available to support a commercial price or Cost-based Pricing Principles will apply. (Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.0.2, Commercial Pricing).

Contract cost principles

Whenever a contract price is negotiated based on costs, the costs will be determined using Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 .

For non-competitive contracts valued at $50,000 and over, with a fixed price or fixed time rate basis of payment, except in cases for the acquisition of commercial goods and services, the price or rate will be negotiated based on the estimated costs computed in accordance with the Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 .

For non-competitive contracts valued at $50,000 or over, with a cost reimbursable basis of payment, except in cases for the acquisition of commercial goods and services, the price will be determined based on actual ¬costs incurred computed in accordance with the Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 .

In both of the above cases, the Contract Cost Principles will be included as a condition of the contract.

Costing Standard

The Costing Standard is located in Section 5.1.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . It supplements Contract Cost Principles by applying principles-based and risk-guided approaches. This facilitates consistency, improves understanding and supports buyers’ professional judgement in determining fair, reasonable, and effective pricing.

Criteria for cost acceptability

For the cost to be acceptable, the contract cost must be attributable, appropriate and reasonable.

A cost is… If it is... attributable incurred directly or indirectly for the fulfillment of the contract, and necessary to fulfil the contract requirements appropriate by its character and nature, representing a cost that is expected to be incurred in the conduct of delivering the contract reasonable by its nature, not exceeding what might be expected to be incurred in the normal delivery of the contract, whether under a competitive or non-competitive procurement

A cost is…

If it is...

attributable

incurred directly or indirectly for the fulfillment of the contract, and

necessary to fulfil the contract requirements

appropriate

by its character and nature, representing a cost that is expected to be incurred in the conduct of delivering the contract

reasonable

by its nature, not exceeding what might be expected to be incurred in the normal delivery of the contract, whether under a competitive or non-competitive procurement

Cost categories and items

The following tables detail specific cost items categorized by cost group, along with their associated subsections within Section 5.1.1, Costing Standard of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . Within the guide subsections, you will find descriptions of each cost item and information on how to assess its acceptability.

Cost accounting practices submission

Where a contract price will be based on estimated or actual costs, the buyer, in consultation with the Procurement Support Services Sector, may require a Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission evidenced by a CAP Submission Acceptance Letter to clarify the cost accounting practices to be used for contract pricing.

The CAP Submission is a formal disclosure of a contractor’s cost accounting practices including the identification of direct and indirect costs and disclosure of methodologies used to allocate indirect costs.

The CAP Submission and the CAP Submission Acceptance Letter (see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.1.2) ensure that the cost accounting practices to be used for contract pricing are clear and understood by all parties by fully disclosing the acceptance of the practices during the pre-contract award period.

For additional information, see the following sections of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing :

Section 5.1.2, Cost Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission, and

Annex 3, Process Steps for Costing Accounting Practices (CAP) Submission

The CAP Submission serves to reinforce the contractor’s obligation to comply with Contract Cost Principles 1031-2. The CAP Submission does not address the reasonableness of the amount of costs but rather the nature of the cost or costing practices.

Costing discussion papers

The following discussion papers help explain costs and guide decisions about which costs are acceptable. They support you in understanding complex issues during contract negotiations and throughout the contract’s lifecycle. They can be found in Annex 4.3 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing :

Annex 4.3.1, Sales and Marketing

Annex 4.3.2, Asset Valuation

Annex 4.3.3, Production Capacity and Indirect Costs Allocation

Costing rates

For methods of allocating the contractor’s fair and reasonable costs to a contract, refer to section 5.1.1.3, Contract Costing Rates of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , which covers:

take-out rates

out-of-plant service rates

mobile repair party rates

material handling rates

purchased labour rates
